channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Engineering] The Pulse: token spend breaks budgets – what next?
item.description:
Gergely Orosz reports that AI coding token spend is rising roughly an order of magnitude at many companies, forcing teams to choose between open-ended adoption, model routing, cheaper defaults, pooled budgets, and stronger ROI measurement.

  • Uncapped agent usage can turn model choice into a material engineering cost, not just a tooling preference.
  • Teams are starting with cheaper default models, high-end model restrictions, monthly spend reporting, and outcome measurement.
  • The real bottleneck is shifting from code generation to review, product direction, and governance of when expensive models are justified.

item.title: [Informatics] The Importance of Native Randomness in CSS
item.description:
Alvaro Montoro explains why native CSS randomization matters: it moves visual variation back into the styling layer instead of pushing layout decisions into JavaScript or build-time tooling.

  • Past CSS randomness patterns were deterministic hacks, build-time freezes, server-side injections, or JavaScript workarounds.
  • CSS Values and Units Level 5 adds `random()` and `random-item()` so the browser can resolve variation inside CSS itself.
  • The useful framing is architectural: use the least powerful web layer that can express the behavior cleanly.

item.title: [Philosophy] Pluralistic: How not to ban surveillance pricing (30 Apr 2026)
item.description:
Cory Doctorow argues that Maryland's surveillance-pricing law is mostly theater because it narrows scope to groceries, preserves consent loopholes, exempts common pricing tactics, and blocks private enforcement.

  • Regulation can fail by design when loopholes are placed in scope, consent, exemptions, and enforcement.
  • Surveillance pricing is framed as a data-driven way to extract more from people with less leverage, not as neutral personalization.
  • The policy lesson transfers to tech governance: read the enforcement mechanism before trusting the headline claim.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] OpenData Buffer: HA pipelines without Kafka
item.description:
OpenData describes a Kafka alternative for telemetry-style ingestion that uses object storage, append-only data files, and a manifest to trade Kafka's general log semantics for lower operational and monthly cost.

  • Telemetry and event pipelines often need durable buffering and at-least-once delivery, not strict ordering or full consumer-group semantics.
  • A narrower protocol over object storage can cut cost sharply when the workload has fixed participants and tolerates sub-second to low-second latency.
  • The tradeoff is less flexibility: throughput, partitioning, participant count, and latency tuning move into explicit system design decisions.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] No, Really, Bash Is Not Enough: Why Large-Scale CI Needs an Orchestrator
item.description:
Ian Duncan argues that shell scripts are fine for linear CI but break down once pipelines need dependency graphs, parallelism, artifact passing, conditional execution, caching, and failure propagation.

  • A mature CI pipeline is a scheduler plus dependency model, not a longer shell script.
  • The article connects practical CI pain to build-system theory: schedulers, rebuilders, traces, and minimal rebuilds are real design choices.
  • Keeping a script entrypoint is compatible with using an orchestrator; the boundary is where coordination logic starts to dominate command execution.
